response:
According to the provided context, the lock-in period for Anchor Investors in an IPO is as follows:

* For public issues, the lock-in period for Anchor Investors is 30 days from the date of allotment (Source: master_circular_for_infrastructure_investment_trusts_invits.pdf, page 10).
* However, for strategic investors, the lock-in period is one year from the date of allotment in the public issue (Source: master_circular_for_infrastructure_investment_trusts_invits.pdf, page 10).

reference: The lock-in period for anchor investors in an IPO is 30 days from the date of allotment.

a) National  Payments  Corporation  of  India  (NPCI):  NPCI,  a  Reserve 
Bank  of  India  (RBI)  initiative,  is  an  umbrella  organization  for  all  retail

Page 16 of 216 
 
b)  Unified Payments Interface (UP I): UPI is an instant payment system 
developed by the NPCI. It enables merging several banking features, 
seamless fund routing & merchant payments into one hood. UPI allows 
instant transfer of money between any two persons’ bank accounts using 
a payment ad dress which uniquely identifies a person's bank account.  
c)  Sponsor Bank:  Sponsor Bank means a Banker to the Issue registered 
with SEBI which is appointed by the Issuer to act as a conduit between the 
Stock Exchanges and NPCI in order to push the mandate col lect requests 
and / or payment instructions of the retail investors into the UPI.
response:
According to Regulation 9(3.1.2.1) of the Regulations, Unpublished Price Sensitive Information (UPSI) refers to any information that may reasonably be expected to affect the price of securities.

This information is not available in the loaded SEBI documents. Please refer to sebi.gov.in for the latest circulars.
reference: UPSI refers to information that is not publicly available and can materially affect the price of securities if disclosed.

response:
According to SEBI (Prohibition of Insider Trading) Regulations, 2015, the disclosure requirements for insiders and their immediate relatives are as follows:

Regulation 6(2) states that a company shall maintain disclosures in physical/electronic mode as per the prescribed format (Annexure 1).

Regulation 8 requires the Code of Fair Disclosure, which includes provisions for timely and accurate disclosure of material information.

Regulation 9 requires the Code of Conduct, which includes provisions for maintaining confidentiality and avoiding insider trading.

The specific details to be disclosed under Regulation 6(2) include:

* Name of the company
* ISIN of the company
* Details of securities held on appointment of Key Managerial Personnel (KMP), Director, Promoter, or Member of the promoter group
* Immediate relatives of such persons and by other such persons as mentioned in Regulation 6(2)

These disclosures are to be made in accordance with Annexure 1, which provides formats for Disclosures under SEBI (Prohibition of Insider Trading) Regulations, 2015.

Please verify with a qualified legal professional regarding compliance deadlines and penalties.
reference: Insider trading regulations require promoters and key personnel to disclose their holdings and trades to ensure transparency.

response:
According to the provided context, a Compliance Officer plays a crucial role in ensuring regulatory compliance within a listed company. The key responsibilities and restrictions on the role of a Compliance Officer are outlined in the following circulars:

1. Master Circular for Compliance with the Provisions of the Securities and Exchange (SEBI) Regulations, 1992:
The Compliance Officer is responsible for ensuring that the listed entity complies with all applicable SEBI regulations. However, the context does not explicitly state the role of a Compliance Officer in relation to day-to-day affairs.

2. Master Circular on ESG Rating Providers - ERPs:
The Compliance Officer is designated by the Manager under Regulation 10(26) of the REIT Regulations and is responsible for ensuring that employees involved in the rating process do not have ownership of the securities of the issuer (as per Section 17.3.4.5).

Please note that the context does not provide a comprehensive overview of the Compliance Officer's role in a listed company.

This information is not available in the loaded SEBI documents. Please refer to sebi.gov.in for the latest circulars.
reference: A compliance officer ensures that the company adheres to SEBI regulations and handles reporting and regulatory filings.

4.2 The listed entity shall ensure that there is no conflict of interest with the assessment

3. ESG Disclosures for value chain  
 
3.1 43[Disclosures for value chain shall be made by the listed company as per BRSR Core, 
as part of its Annual Report. For this purpose, value chain shall encompass the top 
upstream and downstream partners of a listed entity, individually comprising 2% or 
more of the listed entity's purchases and sales (by value) respectively.  However, the 
listed entity may limit disclosure of value chain to cover 75% of its purchases and 
sales (by value) respectively .] 
 
3.2 Listed entities shall report the KPIs in the BRSR Core for their value chain to the extent 
it is attributable to their business with that value chain partner. Such reporting may be
response:
According to Section 3 of the master circular for compliance with the provisions of the Securities and Exchange (SSE) Act, 2015, listed companies are required to make disclosures for their value chain as part of their Annual Report.

Specifically, as per Circular No. SEBI/CIR-NO/ESG/2022/001 dated January 28, 2022, listed entities shall disclose the following:

* Disclosures for value chain shall be made by the listed company as per BRSR Core, as part of its Annual Report.
* Value chain shall encompass the top upstream and downstream partners of a listed entity, individually comprising 2% or more of the listed entity's purchases and sales (by value) respectively. However, the listed entity may limit disclosure of value chain to cover 75% of its purchases and sales (by value) respectively.

Additionally, as per Section 3.1 of the master circular, listed entities shall report the Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) in the BRSR Core for their value chain to the extent it is attributable to their business with that value chain partner.

Please verify with a qualified legal professional for any compliance deadlines or penalties related to ESG disclosure by listed companies.
reference: SEBI requires listed companies to disclose ESG-related information under the Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting (BRSR) framework.

user_input: What is the role of stock exchanges in algorithmic trading?
retrieved_contexts:
market manipulation and market disruptions.  
 
59.2.8.  The stock exchange shall include a report on algorithmic trading on 
the stock exchan ge in the Monthly Development Report (MDR) 
submitted to SEBI inter -alia incorporating turnover details of 
algorithmic trading, algorithmic trading as percentage of total trading, 
number of stock brokers / clients using algorithmic trading, action 
taken in respect of dysfunctional algos, status of grievances, if any, 
received and processed, etc.

59.1.  Any order that is generated using automated execution logic shall be known 
as algorithmic tradi ng. 
 
Guidelines to the stock exchanges and the stock brokers  
 
59.2.  Stock exchanges shall ensure the following while permitting algorithmic 
trading:  
 
59.2.1.  The stock exchange shall have arrangements, procedures and 
system capability to manage the load on their systems  in such a 
manner so as to achieve consistent response time to all stock 
brokers. The stock exchange shall continuously study the 
performance of its systems and, if necessary, undertake system 
upgradation, including periodic upgradation of its surveillance

16.1.2 The stock exchanges shall have arrangements, procedures and system 
capability to manage the load on their systems in such a manner so as to achieve 
consistent response time to all members. The capacity of the trading system of 
the stock exchange should be at least four times the peak order load encountered 
and the Exchange system should be upgraded on a regular basis.  The stock 
exchange shall continuously study the performance of its systems and, if 
necessary, undertake system upgrade, including periodic upgrade of its 
surveillance system, in order to keep pace with the speed of trade and volume of 
data that may arise through algorithmic trading.
response:
According to Section 59.2 of the master circular for stock brokers (page 150), the stock exchange shall include a report on algorithmic trading in the Monthly Development Report (MDR) submitted to SEBI, incorporating details such as turnover, percentage of total trading, number of stock brokers/clients using algorithmic trading, action taken in respect of dysfunctional algorithms, and status of grievances.

Additionally, Section 59.2 states that stock exchanges shall ensure arrangements, procedures, and system capability to manage the load on their systems to achieve consistent response time to all stock brokers (page 149). This includes having a capacity for the trading system that is at least four times the peak order load encountered, with regular upgrades to keep pace with the speed of trade and volume of data arising from algorithmic trading.

Please verify with a qualified legal professional regarding compliance deadlines or penalties.
reference: Stock exchanges monitor algorithmic trading activities to detect manipulation and ensure fair market practices.

response:
The purpose of SEBI (Prevention of Insider Trading) Regulations, 2015 is to prevent unfair market practices and protect investors from potential harm caused by insider trading.

According to Principle 4, the purpose of these regulations is to identify situations that may trigger communication of insider information and ensure compliance with the regulations in such cases.
reference: The purpose of insider trading regulations is to prevent misuse of unpublished price sensitive information and protect investors.

2. Whether the individuals employed as research analyst with a research entity 
are required to obtain registration certificate under RA Regulations?  
No. Individuals employed as research analyst  with a research entity are not required 
to obtain registration certificate fr om S EBI. The research entity, which employs 
individuals as research analysts, is required to obtain registration certificate under the

response:
According to the SEBI master circular for research analysts (page 10), for registration as a research analyst, a research analyst in the form of a limited liability partnership or a body corporate must comply with the qualification and certification requirements provided under the Regulations.

In other words, a research entity that employs individuals as research analysts is required to obtain registration certificate under the RA Regulations. The individuals employed as research analysts are not required to obtain registration certificates from SEBI.

Please verify with a qualified legal professional for any compliance deadlines or penalties related to this requirement.
reference: A research analyst must register with SEBI by meeting qualification criteria and submitting an application with required certifications.

user_input: What is the role of stock brokers in algorithmic trading?
retrieved_contexts:

59.10.2.  The stock broker has procedures and arrangements to safeguard 
algorithms from misus e or unauthorized access.  
 
59.10.3.  The stock broker has real -time monitoring systems to identify 
algorithms that may not behave as expected. Stock broker shall 
keep stock exchange informed of such incidents immediately.  
 
59.10.4.  The stock broker shall maintain logs of all  trading activities to 
facilitate audit trail. The stock broker shall maintain record of 
control parameters, orders, trades and data points emanating 
from trades executed through algorithm trading.  
 
59.10.5.  The stock broker shall inform the stock exchange on any

up to th e level of its clients to the CM.  
 
 CM shall report disaggregated information on 
collaterals up to the level of clients of TM and 
proprietary collaterals of the TMs to the Stock 
Exchanges (SEs) and CCs in respect of each segment.  
13.  59.5 & 59.6 The stock brokers / trading members that provide the 
facility of algorithmic trading shall subject their algorithmic 
trading system to a system audit every six months in order 
to ensure that the requirements prescribed by SEBI / stock 
exchanges with regard to algorithmic trading are effectively 
implemented.  
Deficiencies or issues identified during the process of 
system audit of trading algorithm / software shall be

market manipulation and market disruptions.  
 
59.2.8.  The stock exchange shall include a report on algorithmic trading on 
the stock exchan ge in the Monthly Development Report (MDR) 
submitted to SEBI inter -alia incorporating turnover details of 
algorithmic trading, algorithmic trading as percentage of total trading, 
number of stock brokers / clients using algorithmic trading, action 
taken in respect of dysfunctional algos, status of grievances, if any, 
received and processed, etc.
response:
According to the provided context, the role of stock brokers in algorithmic trading is as follows:

Stock brokers are required to provide the facility of algorithmic trading (Section 13.59.5) and subject their algorithmic trading system to a system audit every six months (Section 13.59.6) to ensure compliance with SEBI/stock exchange requirements.

Additionally, stock brokers must maintain logs of all trading activities (Section 59.10.4), record control parameters, orders, trades, and data points emanating from trades executed through algorithm trading (Section 59.10.4), and inform the stock exchange on any incidents related to algorithms that may not behave as expected (Section 59.10.3).

Stock brokers are also required to keep their clients informed up to the level of their clients about the use of algorithmic trading (Section 13.59.5).
reference: Stock brokers are responsible for ensuring proper risk management and compliance in algorithmic trading activities.

TIMELINES FOR RESOLUTION OF INVESTOR GRIEVANCES IN A PUBLIC ISSUE  
Sr. 
No Activity  No. of calendar 
days  
1 Investor grie vance received by the lead manager  T

response:
The timeline for resolving investor complaints can be found in three separate circulars.

According to the master circular on issue and listing of non-convertible securities securitized (page 178), the lead manager should undertake best efforts to resolve grievances within T+30 days. A desirable indicative timeline is as follows:

1. Investor grievance received by the lead manager: T
2. Lead Manager to offer to identify the concerned intermediary and forward the grievance on T day itself: T+1
3. Investor may escalate the pending grievance, if any, to a senior officer of the lead manager of rank of Vice President or above: T+21

Please verify with a qualified legal professional regarding compliance deadlines.

The master circular also provides a timeline for resolving investor grievances in a public issue (page 173):

1. Investor grievance received by the lead manager: T
2. Lead Manager to offer to identify the concerned intermediary and forward the grievance on T day itself: T+1
3. The concerned intermediary/ies to respond to the lead manager with an acceptable reply: X

Please note that the value of X is not specified in the circular.

The master circular on real estate investment trusts - REITs (page 172) does not provide a separate timeline for resolving investor grievances, but rather provides a general framework for investor grievance redressal.
reference: Investor complaints should typically be resolved within 21 days through SEBIâ€™s grievance redressal mechanism.

and reporting under each principle is divided into essential and leadership indicators.

1.4 The BRSR is intended towards having quantitative and standardized disclosures on 
ESG parameters to enable compara bility across companies, sectors and time. Such 
disclosures will be helpful for investors to make better investment decisions. The 
BRSR shall also enable companies to engage more meaningfully with their

BRSR shall also enable companies to engage more meaningfully with their 
stakeholders, by encouraging them to look beyond fina ncials and towards social and 
environmental impacts.  
 
1.5 The listed entities already preparing and disclosing sustainability reports based on 
internationally accepted reporting frameworks (such as GRI, SASB, TCFD or 
Integrated Reporting) may cross -reference the disclosures made under such 
framework to the disclosures sought under the BRSR.

report. 
 
 
3. Applicability- Some of the disclosur es sought  under the BRSR  may not be applicable to  certain 
industries, say the service industry. In such cases,  the entity can state  that su ch disclosure  is not

4. The term “repor ting period” refers  to the fi nancial year for which BRSR is be ing pre pared . 
 
 
5. The listed entity shou ld endeavour  to provide clear, complete and concise respon ses. 
The web-links to the  relevant do cument may be provided, if availab le . 
 
 
6. The informat ion sought on  complaints in the fo rmat are accompan ied with a column of “Remarks”
response:
According to the context, the purpose of BRSR (Business Responsibility Reporting) reporting is as follows:

The BRSR is intended towards having quantitative and standardized disclosures on ESG parameters to enable comparability across companies, sectors, and time. Such disclosures will be helpful for investors to make better investment decisions.

Additionally, the BRSR shall also enable companies to engage more meaningfully with their stakeholders, by encouraging them to look beyond finances and towards social and environmental impacts.

Please verify with a qualified legal professional for any compliance deadlines or penalties related to this reporting.
reference: BRSR reporting aims to provide disclosures on environmental, social, and governance performance of companies.

SEBI's role in the capital market can be understood from the context provided. According to SEBI, encouraging adoption and usage of financial technology (FinTech) would have a profound impact on the development of securities markets.

As stated in Annexure 26 to the master circular for depositories:

response:
According to the provided context, the role of merchant bankers in an IPO can be summarized as follows:

The merchant bankers are responsible for ensuring that the REIT (or InvIT) fulfills its obligations as disclosed in the offer document and as required by these guidelines. This includes calling upon the REIT, parties to the REIT, directors of the parties to the REIT, or selling unit holders to fulfill their obligations.

In the context of an IPO, the merchant bankers are also responsible for conducting due diligence with regard to the eligibility of IGPIs (Infrastructure Investment Trusts) and ensuring that the holding of the Company in the REIT (or InvIT) is in accordance with Regulation 283(1) of the ICDR Regulations 2018.

Please note that this information is specific to the context provided and may not be comprehensive or up-to-date. For a detailed understanding of the role of merchant bankers in an IPO, it is recommended to refer to the relevant SEBI guidelines and circulars.
reference: Merchant bankers manage the IPO process, ensure regulatory compliance, and assist in issue structuring.

According to Regulation 1(2) of the Listing Regulations, "the primary objective of these Regulations is to promote transparency, accountability and good governance among listed entities."

response:
The role of independent directors, as per SEBI regulations, is outlined in Section 302 of the Securities and Exchange Board of India Act, 1992.

According to the master circular for compliance with the provisions of the Securities and Exchange Board of India Act, 1992 (Page 251), independent directors are expected to:

a. Review the performance of non-independent directors and the Board as a whole.
b. Review the performance of the Chairperson of the company.

Additionally, Section 302(4) of the Securities and Exchange Board of India Act, 1992 states that an independent director shall not be associated with the concerned MF, AMC & its subsidiaries and/or sponsor of AMC during a cooling-off period of 3 years after completing two consecutive terms.

According to Section 19.5.2.7 of the master circular for stock brokers (dated [Date]), a Qualified Stock Broker (QSB) shall employ adequate tools to automate the process of risk management, reporting, and compliance.

Additionally, as per Section 54.5.1, risk management should be comprehensive and take into account the overall positions of clients, irrespective of the medium of trading.

user_input: What is the purpose of market surveillance?
retrieved_contexts:
172 
 
  
62.47.  Stock Brokers should establish appropriate security monitoring systems 
and processes to facilitate continuous monitoring of security events / alerts 
and timely detection of unauthorised  or malicious activities, unauthorised 
changes, unauthorised access and unauthorised copying or transmission 
of data / information held in contractual or fiduciary capacity, by internal and 
external parties. The security logs of systems, applications and n etwork 
devices exposed to the internet should also be monitored for anomalies.  
 
62.48.  Further, to ensure high resilience, high availability and timely detection of

mechanism for prevention and detection of fraud or market abuse125 
 
98.1.  Chapter IVA of the Securities and Exchange Board of India (Stock Brokers) 
(Amendment) Regulations, 2024 (hereinafter referred to as the “Broker 
Regulations”) requires stock brokers to put in place an institutional 
mechanism for prevention and detection of fraud or market abuse. 
Accordingly, it has been decided that stock brokers shall comply with the 
following obligations / mechanisms as laid down in Chapter IVA of the 
Broker Regulations:  
 
98.1.1.  Systems for surveillance of trading activities and internal controls  
98.1.2.  Obligations of the stock broker and its employees

accordance with its risk management procedures.  
 
59.2.6.  The stock exchange may seek details of trading strategies used by 
the algo for such purposes viz. inquiry, surveillance, investigation, 
etc. 
 
59.2.7.  In order to strengthen the surveillance mechanism related to 
algorithmic trading and prevent market manipulation, stock 
exchanges shall take necessary steps to ensure effective monitoring 
and surveillance of orders and trades resulting from trading 
algorithms. Stock exchanges shall periodically review their 
surveillance arrangements in order to better detect and investigate 
market manipulation and market disruptions.
response:
According to Section 62.47 of the master circular for stock brokers, the purpose of market surveillance is to facilitate continuous monitoring of security events/alerts and timely detection of unauthorized or malicious activities, unauthorized changes, unauthorized access, and unauthorized copying or transmission of data/information held in contractual or fiduciary capacity by internal and external parties.

In other words, the primary objective of market surveillance is to ensure that stock brokers establish appropriate security monitoring systems and processes to prevent and detect fraud or market abuse, as required under Chapter IVA of the Securities and Exchange Board of India (Stock Brokers) (Amendment) Regulations, 2024.
reference: Market surveillance aims to detect fraud, manipulation, and ensure fair trading practices.

user_input: What is the function of stock exchanges?
retrieved_contexts:
from the investors’  bank account i.e. the blocking is to be carried out net of discount.  
 
(IV) Role  and Responsibiliti es of the Stock  Exchange(s)  
Role and re sponsibilities  of the Stock  Exchan ges shall include  the following:  
1. The Stock  Exchanges  shall provide file format/  layout  in which  upload/download  shall be made  by 
the various  intermediaries  interacti ng with the stock  exch anges  or amongst them.

16.1.2 The stock exchanges shall have arrangements, procedures and system 
capability to manage the load on their systems in such a manner so as to achieve 
consistent response time to all members. The capacity of the trading system of 
the stock exchange should be at least four times the peak order load encountered 
and the Exchange system should be upgraded on a regular basis.  The stock 
exchange shall continuously study the performance of its systems and, if 
necessary, undertake system upgrade, including periodic upgrade of its 
surveillance system, in order to keep pace with the speed of trade and volume of 
data that may arise through algorithmic trading.

7. The Stock Exchange(s) are directed to:  
  
7.1. bring the provisions of this circular to the notice of the Stock Brokers and also 
disseminate the same on their websites; and  
  
7.2. monitor the operations carried out by an OBPPs.
response:
According to the provided context, the role and responsibilities of the Stock Exchanges include:

1. Providing file format/layout for upload/download by various intermediaries interacting with the stock exchanges or amongst them (Section 1 of Circular on Role and Responsibilities).

Additionally, as per Section 16.1.2 of the Capacity Planning and Real-Time Performance Monitoring Framework for Commodity Trading, the stock exchanges are required to:

- Have arrangements, procedures, and system capability to manage the load on their systems in a manner that achieves consistent response time to all members.
- Ensure the capacity of the trading system is at least four times the peak order load encountered.
- Upgrade the Exchange system regularly, including periodic upgrade of its surveillance system, to keep pace with the speed of trade and volume of data that may arise through algorithmic trading.

Please verify with a qualified legal professional for compliance deadlines or penalties.
reference: Stock exchanges provide a platform for buying and selling securities while ensuring fair and transparent trading.
